text: Today I want to show you a video about left nephrolitherectomy. Unfortunately we have a technical problem with the recording of the sound during the surgery but I think that the case is very interesting because we can show many different anatomical details who are important in this kind of surgery.
The optical port is positioned on the external edge of the rectus abdominis muscle, located one finger below the rib edge on a horizontal line.

It's one finger if you put the finger parallel to the last rib or two tips of fingers. The right part is that four fingers, four fingers for the left also from the optical one, and between the two instruments that I use to work is one hand. It's like an isosceles triangle.
The surgeon discusses the placement of the fourth port, forming a triangle with the existing ports. The fourth port is positioned slightly more to the right than intended, close to the optical port.

When we place the patient, it's placed in a complete lateral position for the left side, and more or less eighty percent when we do a right side, because when we are on the left side, it's important that the patient is positioned to have all the the spleen, the colon and the tail of the pancreas who fell in the midline in one block.

The surgeon encounters adherences near the kidney and carefully releases them, ensuring not to dissect too far to avoid reaching behind the kidney and urethra. The dissection is redirected towards the edge of the colon.

The surgeon begins the section of adherences using a monopolar device, carefully returning closer to the colon edge to avoid deep dissection. The weight of the kidney causes the gas to open the space, revealing the genital fascia near the colon.

The surgeon performs a nephro-ureterectomy, using monopolar cautery to rapidly divide tissue due to the absence of significant vessels. Blunt dissection is used to separate the tissue before cutting the lateral attachments, with the surgeon's graspers creating a visible plane on the left and right.

The surgeon carefully manages small bleeders to maintain a clear surgical view. The left colon is observed to have numerous diverticula, which are fragile and require cautious handling during the procedure.

The surgeon approaches the entrance of the pelvis, encountering significant adhesions likely due to previous inflammation of the colon diverticulum. Care is taken to avoid direct monopolar coagulation on the fragile diverticulum tissue.

The surgeon carefully navigates the anatomical plane, avoiding entry under the peritoneum to prevent bleeding. The colon is released at this level, and efforts are made to free the distal uterus as much as possible.

Of course, if it was a simple nephrectomy, it's not necessary to fight with this adherence inside the pelvis because you are really laterally placed and the access is a bit more difficult it doesn't seem like that but in fact the instruments are completely inside the patient that it means that we have an amplification movement inside which is more important it's for this reason that the movement with the instrument is not so precise because when we move

The surgeon uses a cold knife to carefully release adhesions around the umbilical artery, avoiding coagulation on the nearby diverticulum. The colon and mesocolon are being freed from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon applies traction on the tissue and uses superficial coagulation with the tip of the scissors to incise the tissue. Initially outside the anatomical plane, the surgeon adjusts to return to the correct plane near the iliac vessel within the pelvis. The mesocolon is gently released, and the sigmoid colon is detached using blunt dissection, with the instrument entering the plane and pushing left and right. Diverticulum is visible at this level.

The surgeon continues to dissect the anatomical plane, aiming to free the colon, tail of the pancreas, and spleen en bloc. Traction is applied with the left instrument to separate the tissue plane, while a bipolar grasper is used at an optimal angle to minimize the risk of injuring the diverticulum.

The surgeon methodically dissects the anatomical plane by working incrementally on the left, right, and medial sides. The goal is to fully open the plane and locate the gonadal vein, which serves as a medial landmark. The surgical field is oriented towards the head side of the patient.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection by pushing the instrument left and right to enter the tissue plane, followed by cutting attachments using monopolar coagulation. This technique helps maintain dissection within the atypical plane, minimizing the risk of vessel injury.
The surgeon releases the patellar attachment of the spleen, carefully staying close to the spleen to avoid opening the diaphragm. The peritoneum is visible near the spleen during this maneuver.

The surgeon carefully positions the tip of the bipolar grasping instrument away from the spleen capsule to avoid trauma, instead placing it on the thin peritoneal layers or deeper than the spleen to minimize the risk of injury.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the tail of the pancreas, ensuring the spleen remains isolated. Attention is given to the proximity of the stomach, with a nasogastric tube in place to prevent injury and optimize surgical space.

The surgeon continues to free the mesocolon, gradually dissecting on the feet side and then the head side. The colon and mesocolon are progressively freed, revealing the tail of the pancreas, which appears slightly pink compared to the surrounding fat. The adrenal glands are visible and appear more orange in contrast.

The surgeon differentiates the tail of the pancreas from the adrenal glands based on color, noting the tail of the pancreas is pink and external to the jota fascia, while the adrenal glands are orange and inside the fascia with the kidney. The splenic artery is visible at the tip of the scissors, positioned cranially to the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the splenic vein, noting its thin wall, and prepares to control the splenic artery. The renal vein is identified, and the surgeon moves slightly to the right to avoid direct contact with it, continuing to create space in the surgical field.

The surgeon opens the surgical field to locate the gonadal vein, identifying it within the Girota fascia. Another vein is observed nearby, within the mesocolon, which can be easily confused with the gonadal vein due to their proximity. The gonadal vein is freed sufficiently to ensure access without further dissection of the mesocolon.

The surgeon performs dissection lateral to the gonadal vein to avoid complications with the azigolumbar vein, which is located near the gonadal vein behind the renal vein. The psoas muscle plane is identified, and care is taken to avoid incising too low to prevent missing the uterus.

The surgeon incises vertically under the lower third of the kidney, carefully avoiding the uterus and gonadal vein, and opens the Girota fascia to create a large window for access. Fat tissue is lifted to expose the psoas muscle, ensuring the incision does not reach the kidney capsules.

The surgeon lifts the fat to maintain the dissection plane parallel to the psoas muscle, avoiding parallel alignment with the kidney capsules. Small veins passing from the midline to the kidney are carefully dissected using bipolar grasp and double coagulation, ensuring safe separation of the tissue. The psoas muscle is clearly visible as the surgeon frees it from surrounding fat, moving distally towards the uterus.

The surgeon uses a suction device to lift the uterus, aiming to localize it distally at the level where it crosses the gonadal vein. The dissection involves opening a window in the superficial tissue, potentially encountering a small lower kidney pedicle. A bipolar grasp is used to control any small vessels, approximately two millimeters in diameter, within the tissue.

The surgeon continues careful dissection of the tissue using a bipolar device, revealing the uterus crossing the gonadal vein. The dissection is performed anteriorly before proceeding posteriorly to manage potential bleeding from vessels at the crossing point. The uterus is visible, and the surgeon maintains an external position relative to the gonadal vein.

The surgeon observes the crossing between the uterus and the gonadal vein, noting the potential risks of applying a clip at this stage due to pressure on the urinary tract. The surgical field is viewed through a slightly obscured 3D optical system, requiring occasional cleaning.

The surgeon has successfully mobilized the lower pole of the kidney, freeing the psoas muscle from the posterior part of the kidney. This mobilization provides additional length to the pedicle, facilitating easier dissection. The surgeon prepares to incise the genital fascia, with the vein visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon creates the genital fascia using blunt dissection, gently pushing with the tip of scissors. Monopolar coagulation is applied to manage the Jota fascia, a point of resistance during dissection. The grasp is placed, pushed, opened, and partially closed before moving back to avoid catching tissue behind, ensuring precision and safety in the movement to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon demonstrates a safe dissection technique using a grasping instrument, emphasizing the importance of partial closure to avoid damaging tissue and causing bleeding. The gonadal vein and azigolumbar vein are clearly visible, with the surgeon noting the anatomical relationship between these veins and the renal artery origin.

The surgeon dissects carefully under the azigolombar vein, avoiding proximity to the aorta. Lymphatic tissue and nodes are visible, and the surgeon coagulates these tissues to prevent bleeding. The renal artery is identified, running obliquely from the caudal to the cranial side of the renal vein, and freeing the lymphatic tissue provides additional length for manipulation.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the renal artery, aiming to clean the tissue on its surface while preserving small pieces of lymphatic tissue to maintain elasticity. The renal vein and artery are visible, with the surgeon attempting to access the artery from the opposite side for better exposure.

The surgeon passes around the renal artery and vein, revealing the pulsation of the artery and the surrounding lymphatic tissue. The edge of the artery becomes visible as the surgeon applies pressure to ensure dissection on the artery's surface. Using a bipolar grasp, the surgeon exposes the artery's surface, facilitating easier opening of the tissue plane. A clip is placed to halt the blood flow into the renal artery.

The surgeon uses a hemolog clip to control small bleeders and secure the renal vein, ensuring no issues arise during the procedure. The renal artery is prepared for clipping, with the input flow stopped to create space for complete control. The surgeon checks the positioning and confirms a clear window behind the artery, allowing for precise placement of the clip while avoiding proximity to the aorta.

The surgeon carefully places a Hemolock clip on the posterior surface of the renal vein, ensuring contact between the clip extremities to avoid catching tissue behind. The clip is closed slightly to pass the hook over the vein surface, securing the position. A third clip is placed between the first two clips for added safety before cutting the vein.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the renal artery, carefully controlling and cutting the lymphatic tissue attached around it. This action visibly increases the length of the artery, providing more space for additional clips to be placed. The surgeon ensures that clips are not placed too close to the ostium to avoid distorting the artery from the aorta.

The surgeon carefully places a clip on the renal vein, ensuring it is positioned on the vein itself and not on the vena cava, to prevent slippage during recovery. The pedicle of the kidney is controlled, and a hemolog clip is applied to secure the uterus. The adrenal glands are preserved due to the presence of a TCC, with the suprarenal vein visible.

The surgeon carefully incises along the edge of the adrenal glands, aiming to preserve them during the nephrectomy. The adrenal glands are visible with a distinct color, and the surgeon avoids cutting horizontally through the fat to prevent reaching the kidney capsules.

The surgeon performs an S-shaped incision around the adrenal gland, cutting horizontally along its edge, then vertically behind it, and horizontally again to reach the plane of the psoas muscle. The adrenal gland is grasped, and the incision is made vertically behind it to remove surrounding fat without exposing the kidney capsule. The plane of the psoas muscle is visible as the incision is completed.

The surgeon carefully removes the surrounding fat while preserving the adrenal glands, using a bipolar device for dissection. The assistant lifts the fat from the upper pole of the kidney to ensure clear visibility. The surgeon checks for any large vessels that may require clipping, emphasizing the importance of preventing bleeders during adrenal gland dissection.

The surgeon continues to dissect along the edge of the adrenal glands, occasionally entering the gland and causing minor bleeding, which is controlled with a bipolar device. The incision progresses step by step through the genital fascia, revealing the larger-than-expected adrenal gland extending upwards. The dissection approaches the diaphragm, with caution around the diaphragmatic pedicle to prevent bleeding. Fat is pushed up from the psoas muscle to facilitate the dissection, limiting the volume of tissue needing coagulation and cutting.

The surgeon performs a vertical incision behind the adrenal glands, carefully removing surrounding fat while maintaining the kidney's attachment on the lateral side to prevent it from obstructing the view. The diaphragmatic pedicle is identified, and the kidney remains attached during the dissection of the midline and pedicle, with no main vessels connected.

The surgeon pushes up the kidney and rotates it towards the uterus, controlling any bleeders and freeing the attachments between the psoas muscle and the kidney. The kidney remains attached, facilitating the dissection. The surgeon places a clip to secure the ureter and dissects distally towards the uterus, aiming to ease the removal of the distal uterus from the bladder. The uterus extends deep into the pelvic curvature, making dissection challenging.

The surgeon performs distal dissection of the uterus, ensuring the urinary tract remains closed. The gonadal vein crossing the uterus is addressed, with two clips placed on the uterus before division. The dissection is challenging due to instrument placement optimized for kidney dissection rather than uterus dissection.

The surgeon uses a five millimeter instrument in the dominant right hand to perform precise monopolar coagulation on the lower part of the genital fascia, where a large vein is present. Careful dissection is performed to prevent bleeding, as the lower pole is attached to the uterus, requiring further dissection within the pelvic curvature.

The surgeon uses monopolar and bipolar instruments to dissect distally inside the pelvis, focusing on the gonadal vein and surrounding pedicles. The iliac vessels are visible, and a pedicle crossing them is coagulated with the bipolar instrument. Another pedicle is identified near the bladder, requiring careful coagulation to prevent bleeding from a small artery.

The surgeon methodically releases adhesions around the uterus, stretching it under direct vision. The uterus is seen crossing near the umbilical artery, and the surgeon uses scissors to dissect, applying tension to the tissue to facilitate the approach towards the bladder entrance.

I don't like to control in a laparoscopy the distal part of the uterus because we need to do an incision somewhere to remove the kidney and if the gold standard stays actually the open technique for the distal dissection just for a carcinogical reason. It's important to respect if it's possible I think. Another point is very important in this kind of cancer to never open the urinary tract and also to select well the case. It's important to don't do lap surgery if you think that you have positive nodes potentially because in case of cancer spillage Usually the life expectancy of the patient is six months due to the peritoneal carcinomatosis. Okay, this alley it will be more difficult to do. It will be probably for the open part and we have to release the kidney. You can see also that I left the kidney attached during the dissection of the uterus because it's more easy when it's attached because the uterus is more fixed.

The surgeon releases the kidney by incising the peritoneal attachment, applying traction to rotate the kidney medially at the upper pole. This maneuver reduces resistance, allowing for efficient detachment of the kidney from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon identifies a significant vein within the surgical field, applying traction to open it quickly. The vein, originating from the wall, has branches extending into the joita fascia. The surgeon prepares to control the vein to prevent bleeding, ensuring complete release before coagulation and cutting.

The surgeon uses monopolar coagulation near the diaphragm, ensuring not to inadvertently contract the diaphragm with the scissors. The kidney is now free, and the surgical field is cleaned to check for any significant bleeding. The kidney is repositioned to its anatomical location to facilitate placement into a retrieval bag, especially when dealing with a large kidney.

The surgeon prepares to remove the kidney from the abdomen, ensuring placement of a drain through the peritoneum. The uterus and genital vein are visible, and the surgical field is ready for kidney extraction. A limited incision is made a few centimeters from the anterior iliac crest.

The surgeon opens the abdominal wall layer by layer using monopolar cautery, starting with the skin, followed by the subcutaneous fat and the first aponeurosis. The underlying muscles become visible. The surgeon then uses Mayo scissors to create an opening for the laparoscopic bag, inserting a finger to facilitate entry.

The surgeon uses an undercatch bag with a metal ring to encase the kidney. The bag is opened, and the kidney is maneuvered inside by shaking it along the instrument's axis. The bag is then closed by pulling the string to tighten the metal loop, ensuring the kidney is securely contained.

The surgeon places the drain higher up, near the hilum, and uses traction on the muscle fibers to split them without cutting, allowing the kidney to be removed. The drain is positioned in contact with the hilum, and the surgeon uses two fingers on each side to open the space, facilitating the kidney's extraction.
The kidney is removed from the abdominal cavity. A clip is placed on the uterus to secure it, and the distal uterus is dissected in an open approach using a retractor to maintain access.
